role_summary: Co-founder and captain of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, 1968-1972.
biographical_paragraph: Aaron Dixon was born January 2, 1949 in Chicago, Illinois [src: 1]. In 1968 he co-founded the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, the first chapter established outside California, and served as its captain through 1972 [src: 1]. As captain he established the chapter's Free Breakfast for Children program and helped open a free community medical and legal clinic that continues today as the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center [src: 1]. His oral history is held by the Library of Congress as entry 1 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 2]; his testimony in this archive is in dialogue with that of Kathleen Cleaver (entry 73), whom he first met at Bobby Hutton's funeral in 1968 [src: 1]. Dixon published the memoir "My People Are Rising: Memoir of a Black Panther Party Captain" with Haymarket Books in 2012 [src: 1].

sources.title: Aaron Dixon oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
